body_txt: The Dark Magical Girl is the distortion of The Power of Love and The Power of Friendship , molded into a dark parody of the hero. Where (most) heroes are a force for good and light, the Dark Magical Girl's virtues have all been twisted to serve evil. Most heroes have a good relationship with their family members, they make friends at school — they may be a little dim , but that's okay, and this all translates into the ability to defend what they love from external threats. A Dark Magical Girl doesn't have that. Their parents are absent at best and abusive at worst, and though they're smart, they can't understand why their life has turned out as it is. They don't have real friends or exploits past the academic (if they even go to school) . If they seem outwardly creepy, it's another unfortunate reason people avoid them, which only leads to them being further perceived as evil or otherwise abnormal. They often act as The Dragon to the Big Bad , from whom they may crave approval as a parental figure. Often, their parent is the Big Bad themselves, or at least on the side of villainy, giving them a Freudian Excuse . Inevitably, the Dark Magical Girl will fight the hero, acting as their Evil Counterpart or Shadow Archetype . Their motivation will revolve around craving acceptance and love from the world , fear of being hurt or abandoned, or out of jealousy for the love and friendship the hero has that they do not. They don't want the hero's pity, but the hero will try to reach out to them and gain their friendship regardless. The hero symbolizes the triumph of virtue, justice, and The Power of Love . A Dark Magical Girl asks if there is redemption and salvation for the cursed and lost. A work that falls on the idealist side of the Sliding Scale of Idealism Versus Cynicism will almost always have the DMG converted to the good guys' side by the end, but it is not unheard of for particularly cynical works to kill off the DMG. If they are turned to the side of good, expect the Dark Magical Girl to be fiercely loyal to their newfound friend. The abilities of the Dark Magical Girl are often polar opposites to the hero's. They are also usually faster, smarter, and more ruthless than the hero, making them the primary obstacle to the hero's triumph. Younger Dark Magical Girls tend to be Little Miss Badasses . If they are a Magical Girl Warrior , they're frequently a Lady of War . Dark Magical Girl outfits are often color-coded black or are more sexy than the hero's outfit. As a reminder, the "Dark" in Dark Magical Girl refers to their personality and characterization, not the nature of their powers. In fact, a character whose powers aren't "dark" in nature can still qualify for this trope if they have the right personality and characterization; as mentioned above, usually the driving force behind the actions of a Dark Magical Girl is some combination of their desire for approval from a parental figure, fear of being alone, or jealousy towards everything the hero has. The Dark Magical Girl is a subtrope of Anti-Villain and Shadow Archetype . Almost always The Woobie type on the sliding scale of anti-villains. While female examples are most common, male examples of the trope have become more common over the years. See also Evil Counterpart . Not to be confused with the Black Magician Girl (though there is overlap between these two tropes), the Lady of Black Magic , the Magical Girl Genre Deconstruction (often referred to as "Dark Magical Girl Genre"), or Dark Action Girl . Or Dark Magician Girl for that matter.
